Cardiac rhythm recognition for the sleep lab

Case clue and choices

Clue:
Lead II teaching strip
Use the choices above after you read the rhythm strip.
25 mm/s10 mm/mVLead II

Example only: Third-degree AV block (complete heart block)

AI-generated teaching rendering for AV dissociation. This example is not part of the scored rhythm drill.

25 mm/s 10 mm/mV Lead II example
AI-generated rendering: This original example is included only as a visual teaching aid for AV dissociation and is not part of the scored drill.
Reference materials for more details: review the current AASM Scoring Manual and Sleep Medicine Pearls, 3rd edition, Chapter 15.

How to read the strip

Start with the overall rhythm pattern before using the answer choices above. Use the review button to jump straight to rhythms you missed.

Focus: recognize the rhythm, document the context, and know when patient assessment or escalation matters.
  1. Check rate and regularity first.
  2. Look for P waves and whether they relate to the QRS.
  3. Judge whether the QRS is narrow, wide, early, dropped, or paused.
  4. Then compare your impression to the answer choices above the strip.
Look forWhy it matters
Rate and regularityFast, slow, and irregular patterns narrow the differential quickly.
P waves and PR patternHelpful for sinus rhythm, junctional rhythms, and AV blocks.
QRS width and timingEarly wide beats, broad tachycardias, and ventricular escape patterns stand out here.
Pause or dropped beatImportant when distinguishing sinus pause, AV block, and artifact.
Practice tip: Say it out loud before answering: regular or irregular, narrow or wide, P waves yes or no, pause yes or no.

Disclaimer

This page is for educational rhythm-recognition practice only. Any example images or rhythm renderings shown here are AI-generated teaching renderings or likenesses, not official diagnostic reference images. Always refer to BRPT-approved reference materials for more detailed images and information. This page is not clinical interpretation, medical advice, or emergency direction. Real patient care decisions should follow your lab policy, supervising clinician guidance, and emergency procedures.
